twitter.hook: 740 million adults worldwide have tinnitus. $26 billion in annual economic costs. In 2023, the FDA cleared the first-ever device for it — Lenire. In 2016, Lipo-Flavonoid failed its one adequately designed placebo-controlled trial. It's still on pharmacy shelves. Here's the honest 2026 map.
thread: Lenire — March 2023, FDA De Novo Class II authorization, the first device clearance ever for tinnitus. It delivers synchronized sound to the ears and mild electrical stimulation to the tongue via a mouthpiece. The hypothesis: pairing auditory and somatosensory input triggers neuroplasticity in the auditory cortex.

The evidence trail: TENT-A1 (Conlon et al., Science Translational Medicine, 2020) — 326 participants, 86.2% showed THI improvement, 80.1% maintained it at 12-month follow-up. TENT-A2 (Scientific Reports, 2022) — average THI reduction 18.5 points at 12 weeks, 20.2 at 12-month follow-up without further treatment.

TENT-A3 (Nature Communications, 2024): 112 participants, three independent sites, active control. Bimodal arm outperformed. 79.4% of patients experienced clinically significant improvement. Real-world evidence from 204 additional patients: mean THI improvement 27.8 points.

The THI context: the field considers a 6-7 point reduction the minimal clinically important difference. Lenire's three trials averaged 18-28 points. That's substantial. De Novo authorization is not full FDA approval — it establishes a new device category for novel technologies with real evidence.

The access finding that matters as much as the device: Beukes et al. (JAMA Otolaryngology, 2018) — internet-delivered CBT was non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress in a head-to-head RCT. For the 90% of tinnitus sufferers without specialist access, this is the most important clinical development in years.

What CBT does and doesn't do: Hesser et al. (Clinical Psychology Review, 2011) — 15 studies, 1,091 participants, Hedges' g = 0.70 on tinnitus distress. Not on loudness. This distinction is everything and is routinely erased in marketing. The ringing stays. Its grip on daily life loosens.

Lipo-Flavonoid has been marketed for tinnitus for 60+ years. In that time, ONE adequately designed placebo-controlled RCT was run. Le et al. (Otolaryngology-HNS, 2016): no significant improvement in either active arm. AAO-HNS does not recommend it. American Tinnitus Association does not recommend it. It's still in every pharmacy.

Ginkgo biloba, Tinnitus 911, Ring Ease: zero qualifying controlled trials. A 2018 literature review by Park & Moon was titled 'Over-the-Counter Tinnitus Cures: Marketers' Promises Do Not Ring True.' The European Food Safety Authority banned health claims for lemon bioflavonoids. The U.S. market has no equivalent restriction.

Why: supplements are regulated as food, not drugs. No proof of efficacy required before sale. The tinnitus supplement market is not a conspiracy — it is a regulatory arbitrage filling a gap where validated treatments cost $3,000-$5,000 and require specialist access most patients can't reach.

What the evidence actually establishes: tinnitus distress is treatable. Tinnitus loudness reduction is rare. Access to validated interventions is still limited by cost, geography, and the regulatory gap that keeps placebo supplements on pharmacy shelves.
twitter.cta: The full 2023-2026 evidence map — Lenire, iCBT, and why supplements don't work: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/tinnitus-evidence-2026 — Share if someone you know is spending $60/month on Lipo-Flavonoid.

linkedin.hook:
740 million adults have tinnitus. The supplement industry has sold them bioflavonoid pills for 60 years.

In 2016, Lipo-Flavonoid — the category's flagship product — failed its one adequately designed placebo-controlled trial.

In March 2023, a device called Lenire received the first-ever FDA clearance for tinnitus, with 79.4% of participants showing clinically significant improvement.

The gap between those two things is worth examining.
linkedin.body:
The tinnitus evidence landscape in 2026 is more advanced than most practitioners and patients know — and the validated options are largely inaccessible to most people who need them.

What changed in 2023-2024:

Lenire received FDA De Novo Class II authorization in March 2023 — the first regulatory clearance of any device for tinnitus. It uses bimodal stimulation: synchronized sound plus mild electrical pulses to the tongue. Three controlled trials (TENT-A1, A2, A3) documented average THI reductions of 18-28 points, well above the 6-7 point minimal clinically important difference. Effects appear durable at 12-month follow-up without continued treatment. Cost: approximately $3,000-$5,000. Available at 100+ U.S. audiology clinics.

Internet-delivered CBT was confirmed non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress in a head-to-head RCT (Beukes et al., JAMA Otolaryngology, 2018). For the 90% of tinnitus sufferers without realistic access to specialist audiologists, this is potentially the most impactful finding in the recent literature. iCBT is not freely available — but the cost and access barriers are dramatically lower than in-person clinical care.

What the evidence establishes about CBT: Hedges' g = 0.70 on tinnitus distress versus passive control (Hesser et al., Clinical Psychology Review, 2011, 15 studies, 1,091 participants). Not on loudness. CBT reduces how much the ringing controls daily life. It does not reduce how loud the ringing is. This distinction is clinically essential and routinely conflated in product marketing.

What the evidence says about supplements:

Lipo-Flavonoid (60+ years on the market): one adequately designed placebo-controlled RCT (Le et al., Otolaryngology-HNS, 2016). Result: no significant improvement. AAO-HNS does not recommend it. American Tinnitus Association does not recommend it. It remains on shelves because supplements don't require efficacy evidence before sale.

Ginkgo biloba, branded 'Tinnitus 911' formulations: zero qualifying controlled trials.

The question worth asking: why do the interventions with documented clinical evidence remain behind specialist-access walls and $3,000-$5,000 price points, while the interventions with no evidence fill pharmacy aisles at $60/month?

Full citation-backed analysis: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/tinnitus-evidence-2026

slides.title: SLIDE 1 — HOOK
slides.body: 740 million adults. $26 billion annual economic cost. The FDA cleared the first tinnitus device in 2023. Lipo-Flavonoid failed its one RCT in 2016. It's still on pharmacy shelves.

slides.title: SLIDE 2 — STAT
slides.body: Lenire TENT-A2 (Conlon et al., Scientific Reports 2022, n=298): average THI reduction 18.5 points at 12 weeks — sustained at 20.2 points after 12 months without further treatment. Field threshold for meaningful: 6-7 points.

slides.title: SLIDE 3 — REVEAL
slides.body: Beukes et al., JAMA Otolaryngology 2018: internet-delivered CBT was non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress. For the 90% without specialist access, this is the most important tinnitus finding in years.

slides.title: SLIDE 4 — DETAIL
slides.body: Hesser et al., Clinical Psychology Review 2011 (15 studies, n=1,091): CBT achieves Hedges' g = 0.70 on tinnitus distress. Not on loudness. The ringing stays. Its grip on your life loosens. That distinction matters.

slides.title: SLIDE 5 — QUOTE
slides.body: "Over-the-Counter Tinnitus Cures: Marketers' Promises Do Not Ring True." — Title of Park & Moon's 2018 Otolaryngology review of OTC tinnitus supplement evidence

slides.title: SLIDE 6 — IMPLICATION
slides.body: The interventions with documented evidence cost $3,000-$5,000 and require specialist access. The ones with no evidence cost $60/month and fill every pharmacy. The gap is regulatory, not scientific.

slides.title: SLIDE 7 — SOURCES
slides.body: Conlon et al., Science Translational Medicine (2020) · Beukes et al., JAMA Otolaryngology (2018) · Hesser et al., Clinical Psychology Review (2011) · Le et al., Otolaryngology-HNS (2016) · Haider et al., JAMA Neurology (2022)

reddit.title: What does the 2023-2026 evidence actually show — Lenire, iCBT, VNS, and why every OTC supplement you've tried didn't work
reddit.body:
Been going through the tinnitus literature systematically and wanted to share what the peer-reviewed record actually says vs. what you find on supplement websites.

WHAT HAS ACTUAL EVIDENCE (2023-2026):

1. Lenire (bimodal: sound + tongue stimulation)
FDA De Novo Class II authorized March 2023 — first-ever device clearance for tinnitus.
Three trials: TENT-A1 (Science Translational Medicine 2020, n=326), TENT-A2 (Scientific Reports 2022, n=298), TENT-A3 (Nature Communications 2024).
Average THI reduction: 18.5-27.8 points across trials. Field threshold for clinically meaningful: 6-7 points. Durable at 12-month follow-up without continued treatment.
Cost: ~$3,000-$5,000. Available at 100+ US audiology clinics.

2. Internet-delivered CBT (iCBT)
Beukes et al. (JAMA Otolaryngology, 2018): RCT showing iCBT non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress. Access-changing if you can't get to a specialist.

3. CBT (any delivery)
Hesser et al. (Clinical Psychology Review, 2011, 15 studies, n=1,091): Hedges' g = 0.70 on distress. NOT on loudness. The ringing doesn't get quieter. The suffering from it does. This distinction matters.

EARLY/INCONCLUSIVE:
- VNS paired with tones: Tyler et al. (Scientific Reports, 2017), n=30, 50% responder rate. Early pilot. Surgical implant. Not clinic-ready.
- rTMS: inconsistent across trials, no consensus protocol, multiple Cochrane-adjacent reviews unable to recommend.

NO EVIDENCE AT ALL:
- Lipo-Flavonoid: one adequately designed RCT (Le et al., 2016) — no significant improvement. AAO-HNS and ATA do not recommend it.
- Ginkgo biloba: Cochrane-adjacent systematic review: no clear benefit in adequately powered studies.
- Tinnitus 911, Ring Ease, herbal complexes: zero qualifying controlled trials.

Full breakdown: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/tinnitus-evidence-2026

reddit.title: Lipo-Flavonoid for tinnitus: one adequately designed RCT (2016), no significant effect, still on pharmacy shelves — why?
reddit.body:
Posting this specifically because Lipo-Flavonoid still comes up regularly in tinnitus threads as something worth trying.

The evidence history:
Lipo-Flavonoid (eriodictyol glycoside, bioflavonoids derived from lemon) has been marketed for tinnitus in the U.S. for 60+ years. In that entire time, exactly one adequately designed, placebo-controlled randomized controlled trial has been conducted.

The study: Le et al. (Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery, 2016). Two active arms — manganese and Lipoflavonoid Plus. Neither showed statistically significant improvement in tinnitus symptoms versus placebo.

The professional positions:
- American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery: does not recommend Lipo-Flavonoid for tinnitus
- American Tinnitus Association: does not recommend Lipo-Flavonoid for tinnitus

Why is it still on shelves?
Supplements in the U.S. are regulated as food, not drugs. The FDA does not require efficacy evidence before a supplement can be sold. 'Supports healthy hearing' is not the same legal claim as 'treats tinnitus' — and supplement labels are written accordingly.

The European Food Safety Authority has banned health claims for lemon bioflavonoids due to insufficient evidence. The U.S. market has no equivalent restriction.

For what actually has peer-reviewed evidence: Lenire (FDA De Novo authorized March 2023, 3 trials, average THI reduction 18-28 points) and CBT/iCBT (Hesser meta-analysis: g = 0.70 on distress).

Full 2026 evidence map: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/tinnitus-evidence-2026

email.subject: First FDA-cleared tinnitus device: what 3 trials show
email.preheader: 740 million adults. Lenire cleared in 2023. Lipo-Flavonoid failed in 2016. Still on every shelf.
email.body:
In March 2023, the FDA granted De Novo Class II authorization to Lenire — the first device clearance ever issued for tinnitus.

Three controlled trials, 18-28 point average THI reductions (field threshold for meaningful: 6-7 points). Durable at 12-month follow-up. 79.4% of TENT-A3 participants showed clinically significant improvement.

That same year, Lipo-Flavonoid — marketed for tinnitus for 60+ years — remained on pharmacy shelves after its one adequately designed RCT (Le et al., 2016) found no significant effect in either active arm.

The Beukes 2018 finding: internet-delivered CBT was non-inferior to in-person CBT for tinnitus distress. For the 90% of sufferers without specialist access, this may matter more than any device.

What CBT does: reduces distress (g = 0.70). What it does not do: reduce loudness. The ringing stays. Its grip on your life loosens.

Full 2026 evidence map: https://www.blackswanp.com/research/mental-work/tinnitus-evidence-2026

Subscribe for one transmission per week.

pullQuotes: Lipo-Flavonoid: 60 years on shelves, one RCT, no significant effect.
Lenire TENT-A2: 18.5-point THI reduction — durable at 12 months without further treatment.
iCBT was non-inferior to in-person CBT. The 90% without specialist access now have an option.
CBT reduces tinnitus distress. Not loudness. That distinction is everything.
740 million adults. The interventions with evidence cost $3,000. Placebos cost $60/month.
